main_summary.summary: William Lucy, longtime Secretary-Treasurer of the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME), gives a June 25, 2013 oral history to Emilye Crosby in Washington, DC about his path from Contra Costa County, California civil-service engineering aid to a central staff role in the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike and subsequent labor and anti-apartheid organizing. Born in Memphis under the political machine of Edward Hull "E.H." Crump, Lucy moved to Richmond, California in March 1942 when his father was recruited to Kaiser Shipyards during the World War II mobilization. After Mare Island Naval Shipyard and thirteen years at Contra Costa County (rising to system materials and research engineer), he was recruited in 1966 by AFSCME's new pro-union president Jerry Wurf to direct Legislation and Community Affairs. Sent to Memphis in February 1968 after two sanitation workers were crushed in a packer-truck mechanism, Lucy describes the 67-day strike, the "I Am a Man" slogan he helped formulate at the Peabody Hotel with a local minister, Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's role, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr's two visits and assassination on April 4, the failed earlier settlement that collapsed under the "Mayor Capitulates" headline, and Lewis Donelson's one-vote-short city council effort. He covers the founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists (1972), the Free South Africa Movement (1984), and bringing Nelson Mandela on his 1990 U.S. tour.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Lucy is a primary-source narrator on the operational interior of the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike: who was in the room with Mayor Henry Loeb III, how the "I Am a Man" sign was drafted in the Peabody Hotel from Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's earlier press-conference framing, why the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover and provocateurs at the disrupted March 28 march figured into King's decision-making, and the mechanics of the federal mediation by Under Secretary of Labor James J. Reynolds and Frank Miles that finally produced settlement money from industrialist Abe Plough rather than the city budget.

The interview also documents the institutional history of Black labor organizing post-strike: the 1972 founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a response to the AFL-CIO's neutrality between McGovern and Nixon, the 1984 launch of the Free South Africa Movement under Randall Robinson and TransAfrica, and the 1990 Mandela U.S. tour. As one of the few surviving senior strategists from both the Memphis strike staff and the post-Memphis Black-labor institutional build-out, Lucy's testimony connects the Civil Rights Movement's economic-justice arc to subsequent decades of trade-union and anti-apartheid work.

chapters.title: Recruited for the War Effort
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Smithsonian offices in June 2013, William Lucy describes being born in Memphis to parents from Catherine and Uniontown, Alabama, and his family's 1942 move to the Bay Area after his father, a Memphis Light, Gas and Water worker, was recruited along with many others to work for the Kaiser shipyards.

chapters.title: Everybody Lived Together
chapters.summary: Lucy recalls growing up in wartime defense housing in Richmond, California, where Mexican American and African American families lived in the same projects and nearly everyone worked for Standard Oil, Ford, or Kaiser, and contrasts the segregated but good schooling there with the harsher segregation of Memphis.

chapters.title: From Unskilled Laborer to Journeyman Welder
chapters.summary: Lucy describes his largely self-taught father, who became a journeyman welder six weeks after starting at Kaiser and later opened his own mechanic shop, his seamstress mother who ran a restaurant, his older brother Joe Lucy Jr., and a typical youth of baseball, basketball, and football culminating at El Cerrito High School.

chapters.title: Materials and Research at the County
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts working at Mare Island Naval Shipyard, then joining Contra Costa County's engineering department in 1953, studying materials research at the University of California at Berkeley to advance his career, and rising over thirteen years from entry-level engineering aid to the county's systems materials and research engineer.

chapters.title: Why Are We Not a Union
chapters.summary: Lucy explains how, invited to join an employee association at the county, he came to believe the civil service system had overstepped its mandate by setting wages and benefits, how the membership voted after a year-long forum to become a collective bargaining union, and credits two old trade unionists, Ben Russell and the blacksmith Red Aiello, with shaping his conviction that workers had a right to a voice.

chapters.title: A Bus Driver in Two Sectors
chapters.summary: Lucy describes the fight to organize public employees, whom Franklin D. Roosevelt had excluded from the National Labor Relations Act, his argument that a public and a private bus driver deserve the same rights, and how the group drew the attention of AFSCME as reformer Jerry Wurf challenged longtime president Arnold S. Zander.

chapters.title: Out of My Zip Code One Time
chapters.summary: Lucy tells how, after Wurf won AFSCME's leadership in 1964, he was asked to join the national staff, hesitated because he had only left his zip code once, and in June 1966 left county service for Washington as Associate Director of the Department of Legislation and Community Affairs, believing a union could be a vehicle for social change.

chapters.title: Chavez, Itliong, and the Grapes
chapters.summary: Lucy connects his union work to the wider freedom struggle, recalling the United Farm Workers organizing led by Cesar Chavez and Larry Itliong against Gallo and the grape industry, and his view that both the farm workers' fight and the Southern civil rights struggle were fundamentally about economic exploitation.

chapters.title: A Floor, Not a Ceiling
chapters.summary: Lucy describes early department victories, securing survivor benefits for descendants of Panama Canal construction workers and extending minimum wage coverage under the interstate commerce clause to private hospital and nursing home workers, and argues the minimum wage can be both a blessing and a curse for low-wage workers.

chapters.title: The Same Rights as Anyone
chapters.summary: Lucy recalls Al Bilik as an influence in Washington and lays out the union's case that public employees deserved the same right to organize and bargain as private-sector workers, a presentation so new that public workers joined by the thousands even though few states had laws protecting the process.

chapters.title: Crushed in the Back of the Truck
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts how in 1968 Jerry Wurf sent him from Detroit to look at unrest in the Memphis sanitation department, where men earned wages low enough to qualify for every poverty program, and describes the standoff with newly elected Mayor Henry Loeb III and the catalyst that pushed frustrated workers to strike: two men crushed to death by a garbage truck's packing mechanism.

chapters.title: A City With Power, Workers With an Idea
chapters.summary: Lucy tells of returning from Detroit to Memphis to work alongside organizers Jesse Epps and Joe Paisley, the strike growing into a sixty-seven-day classic confrontation between a city with total power and workers with only a good idea, and how Martin Luther King Jr. came to see it as a great contradiction.

chapters.title: More Important Than a Lawyer in July
chapters.topic: The Hidden Value of Sanitation Work
chapters.summary: Lucy explains the early effort to keep the strike from becoming a racial polarization and how King made the analogy of people who work every day yet stay in poverty, with 85 to 90 percent of the workers qualifying for public aid, and recounts a Westchester County radio caller he answered by noting that by July a sanitation worker matters more than a lawyer for public health.

chapters.title: A Leaflet at Every Home
chapters.summary: Lucy describes asking the strikers to talk to their wives and leave a leaflet at every home they serviced, winning solid support in the African American community and lukewarm support among whites who believed the National had come to take advantage of the city, a suspicion fostered by the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover.

chapters.title: From Boy to Uncle to Grandpa
chapters.topic: The Sign and the Women's Role
chapters.summary: Lucy reflects that beyond wages and safety the core of the strike was a demand for respect and dignity, and recounts how he and a local minister, working in the Peabody Hotel from a phrase by Reverend James M. Lawson Jr., produced the blocked-letter sign reading I Am a Man, which defined the struggle for men who had gone from boy to uncle to grandpa without ever being called men, and insists the strike could not have succeeded without the women.

chapters.title: The Most Hated Man in Memphis
chapters.topic: Reverend Lawson and the Black Church
chapters.summary: Lucy portrays Reverend James M. Lawson Jr., a longtime ally of King who had studied Gandhi's tactics and became known as the most hated African American in Memphis, and complicates the story that the Black church joined instantly, noting the church had little union experience and took time to grasp the issues.

chapters.title: The Ultimate Contradiction
chapters.summary: Lucy recalls King aligning the strike with the Poor People's Campaign and coming to Memphis twice, his second visit ending in his April 4 assassination after the I've Been to the Mountaintop speech, his doubt that James Earl Ray acted alone, and the violence on the day of King's last march, which he argues, citing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, was largely the work of provocateurs rather than the community-minded young Invaders.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: There Has to Be Another March
chapters.topic: Managing the Strike After the Killing
chapters.summary: Lucy describes how the violence polarized the city and brought in the National Guard, his single meeting with King at the Rivermont Hotel to analyze the broken-up march, the decision that there had to be another march, and the work after the assassination of containing street violence and building a sense of protection for strikers facing an openly hostile police force.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: A Mediator From Washington
chapters.summary: Lucy describes how, after the memorial service, President Lyndon Johnson assigned mediator James J. Reynolds, who brought in Frank Miles, and how the strike came to be seen as a civil rights issue even before King arrived, with well-to-do white women visiting sanitation workers' homes and pressure mounting on the stonewalling mayor.

chapters.title: Mayor Capitulates
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts the strike being resolved twice before it finally ended: a recognition clause worked out with city attorney Janati that Loeb rejected, and a later agreement that collapsed when someone leaked it to the press under the headline Mayor Capitulates, after which Loeb, watching his poll numbers, walked back from the table for good.

chapters.title: Abe Plough Puts Up the Money
chapters.summary: Lucy tells of councilman Lewis Donelson coming one vote short of forcing a settlement and blaming himself for King's assassination, the final agreement moving through the city council with industrialist Abe Plough quietly putting up the settlement money, and the irony of Loeb later joining a farmers' tractor protest.

chapters.title: A Position of Neutrality
chapters.summary: Lucy turns to the founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, born as a reaction to the AFL-CIO's 1972 decision to stay neutral between George McGovern and Richard Nixon despite Nixon's record, and recalls how he and colleagues Nelson Jack Edwards, Charles A. Hayes, Cleveland Robinson, and William H. Simons called a Chicago meeting by word of mouth where 1,300 trade unionists unexpectedly showed up.

chapters.title: From a Black Perspective
chapters.topic: Naming the Coalition and Its Impact
chapters.summary: Lucy describes taking a year to consult workers and labor leaders before a second convention voted, with malice aforethought, to name the body the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, open to all but addressing issues from a Black perspective, and credits it with helping diversify the AFL-CIO executive council, once nearly all white but for A. Philip Randolph and C.L. Dellums, into a body of full partners.

chapters.title: A Special Brand of Evil
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts how the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, holding apartheid to be an abomination, began around 1984 to challenge a free labor movement working alongside it, partnering with Randall Robinson's TransAfrica to put Black thought into foreign policy and forming the Free South Africa Movement under President Reagan.

chapters.title: Twenty-Seven Years in Prison
chapters.summary: Lucy describes Nelson Mandela's eventual release and presidency and explains the dual purpose of Mandela's 1990 tour, to let him project his vision of a non-racial South Africa and to let the country see a man who had spent twenty-seven years in prison for that thought, and reflects on America's long accommodation of apartheid.

chapters.title: The Problem of Our Country Is Race
chapters.summary: Lucy closes by weighing whether America needs a truth and reconciliation commission like South Africa's, arguing for mandatory voting to expose the divisions over who should participate in democracy, and naming race, carried into the immigration debate over who was here first, as the enduring problem of the country.

loc_video.caption: William Lucy oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Washington, D.C., 2013 June 25
